criteria: Timer-based sampling at configurable frequency using ITIMER_PROF with SIGPROF signal delivery
Stack unwinding via frame pointer chaining captures full call stack up to 128 frames
Samples aggregated by folded call path with thread attribution using atomic ring buffer
Signal handler is async-signal-safe: no memory allocation, no locks, only atomic operations and pre-allocated buffers
Overhead measurement infrastructure with start/stop timing and sample counting
Multi-threaded support via global timer with per-thread identification using gettid syscall
Dynamic library handling through /proc/self/maps parsing with ASLR-adjusted symbol resolution
Symbol table loaded from ELF binaries with address-to-function-name lookup
Prime frequency configuration (99Hz default) prevents lock-step sampling bias
Alternate signal stack (sigaltstack) prevents stack overflow during signal handling
Export collapsed stack format for downstream flame graph generation
CLI interface for starting, stopping, and printing profiler reports
Call graph constructed from stack samples with nodes containing function name, total_samples, self_samples, caller edges, and callee edges
Stack folding implemented to aggregate identical call paths into collapsed stack format (path;to;function count)
Flame graph tree structure built from collapsed stacks with hierarchical parent-child relationships
SVG flame graph generation with configurable width, height-per-frame, and color scheme based on function name hashing
JSON export in speedscope format for integration with external visualization tools
Function filtering to create subgraphs containing only paths through specified functions
Differential flame graph comparison showing baseline vs comparison sample counts with percentage change calculation
Recursive function handling with self-edges in call graph and cumulative width in flame graphs
Hottest path analysis to identify the call path contributing most samples to a target function
Self time vs total time distinction in call graph nodes for accurate attribution
Implement async task identification using unique task IDs assigned at spawn time with thread-local tracking of current task context
Reconstruct logical call stacks by tracking parent-child task relationships and spawn contexts across await points
Separate and track CPU time (poll duration) vs await time (gaps between polls) with per-task time breakdown
Integrate with Tokio runtime via custom task wrapper that instruments poll() lifecycle with begin/end tracking
Generate async-aware flame graph data that distinguishes CPU frames from await time visualization
Handle task spawning with spawn stack capture and relationship graph construction for parent-child tracking
Detect and report thread migration by tracking which threads each task has executed on
Implement task completion tracking with total lifecycle time from spawn to completion
Provide time breakdown categorization distinguishing I/O wait, scheduling delays, and unknown await time
Generate comprehensive async profiling report with CPU hot paths, task summaries, and time distribution
